item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Somehow, the idea of aborted baby Rock'em Sock'em Robots got 10x funnier now that I know it's up agains Cain.

You should probably croslink that, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-073, also known as Cain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dude, a short month later, The Great RPC Retcon already kicked in. They're saying they left because of "authorial freedom" and "returning to roots". Ive spent enough time fighting the Class 1 Tarterian Entities coming out of r/thedonald to know a dogwhistle when I hear one.

The real irony is that I have never actually seen politics come up here, which is pretty impressive since that's all writers ever seem to talk about.

Oh, and for the stupid people on this site,1 who's the cyborg?

Footnotes
1. that totally don't include me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"How dare you infringe on my freedom of speech you SJW! I'm moving this and all my other SCPs over to the Rock Punching Center Authority right now!"

In all seriousness, I understand that, but SCP files intentionally objectify their subjects, describing them as coldly and clinically as possible. Plus, these little guys aren't legally people, they're homunculi made from resurrected aborted fetuses. All around, it's a pretty bad situation as far as political correctness is concerned.

On a related note, a moment of silence for the SCPS Solidarity. I really liked that one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "hermaphroditic" is not considered a proper and respectful term to use for human individuals with such conditions. "Intersex" would be the preferred word to use.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You guys have a way with writing characters I swear. 1+

…because this well-deserved threshold has yet to be congratulated.

Also, seeing as how I neglected to comment the first time around:

Legitimately fun character interactions and interesting backstories like the one on display here are a bit of a rarity in SCP articles themselves. I feel oftentimes they become a slog, as authors can sometimes focus more on exposition at the character's expense.

This is very not much the case here.

It walks unicycles the fine tightrope between revealing character and progressing narrative, and all the while remaining thoroughly entertaining. This is the Circus at its best, and my only complaint is that not enough people have been exposed to it. +1'd a while ago, but would do again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I feel the less tasteful elements of this article might've been mitigated by some further emotional foray into the implications of such a utterly horrific scenario, I think any IC acknowledgement of the abuse perpetrated would absolutely kill the levity you've created here.

I was worried the human element may end up getting lost in all the absurdity, but I think the more melancholic lines toward the end added just the right amount of weight to the situation without completely bringing the mood down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
YEEESSSS. It's about time we got some pickled punks added to the Circus lore. The fact that they're Fuller's own children is the perfect grimdark cherry on top.

I want my mummy, indeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd normally find such a breakneck change in tone quite irritating, but here I think it hits that sweet spot of dark comedy that so many articles fall short of.

SCP-3717-01: I demand a lawyer, or an actor who’s so method they actually got a law degree!

Favourite line. You've done very good work here. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, this is wonderful. Just wonderful.

In just one interview SCP-3717 became some of my favourite characters on the site. The background details about the inner workings of Herman's Circus (and Herman himself) was also very intriguing.

If I had one complaint, it's that “AH! Sideshow Bob!” sounds too silly for a taskforce name. But I created a taskforce called "Greendale Humans" so… (shrugs).

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
King Frederick III of Denmark collected them. Thousands of them.

Not as strange as it may seem. Curiosity collections were highly prized at the time as both a status symbol and as a research tool for naturalists and doctors, so it isn't surprising that a king who founded one of the greatest libraries on Earth had one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just remembered where I first learned about these - Bradbury's short story "The Jar", which is in the "October Country" collection. Just as disturbing as you'd expect.

More disturbing - King Frederick III of Denmark collected them. Thousands of them. I haven't found out what happened to them after he died.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given the tone of the interview, I'd honestly prefer if this was a full -J than a mainlister. Still, it's very entertaining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somebody please write the fight scene between the mech suit and the cyborg. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, good to see this posted. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show whatever
- Hide whatever

DNA analysis of SCP-3717 has revealed that they are genetically human as well as half-siblings, all possessing the same father.

o boy

Herman would say that after having some fun with a lady friend sometimes they'd have an accident

aw geez

our alchemical consultant, and after a little razzmatazz the lady would have no cause to pester him

he goin for it

The aborted bastards of Herman Fuller, dear old dad.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cwM90OSFodA

This fucks me up. It really does. I am unsure how to feel about this.

On the one hand, it's funny. Very funny. Funny in such a way that in a prior version of this review I made some passing reference to Mel Brooks, and any comparison to Mel Brooks, whether positive or negative means in the eyes of someone you're in the company of gods.

On the other, this is so disturbing, so incomprehensibly awful I feel ashamed - like, legitimately ashamed, as in right now I feel a little nauseated at myself - for enjoying this. I'm at something of a loss for words. While I feel the less tasteful elements of this article might've been mitigated by some further emotional foray into the implications of such a utterly horrific scenario, I think any IC acknowledgement of the abuse perpetrated would absolutely kill the levity you've created here. So here's what I've got:

I also think, on some off-kilter, genius level this article actually perfectly articulates these inanely tragic events in a way that is the complete, polar opposite of what they entail. And that's pretty brilliant. Certainly you've could've headed in the direction of something resembling The Children (of course an excellent piece in its own right) but I feel the tone manages to give it its own voice and also focus on the - I don't want to call a positive angle, because, no. It's Fuller's angle - his own fucked up way of changing the world to suit his whims and fix his problems, which is hell for most and heaven for others.

That's pretty much it. I liked this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pathfinder monster is named for the old sideshow term.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
……So no one's going to mention that 'Pickled Punk' is a monster in Pathfinder?

This is a pretty decent article writing-wise, surprisingly, and even legitimately sort of funny. That said, I don't think I'll be able to vote, mostly because it's just not my kind of thing but also due to the above making me side-eye a little.

item.title: Soon
item.content:encoded:
The reminder that Fuller is no longer part of the Circus also points up the fact that (as far as I can recall) we've never actually learned how or why that happened.

No images in the forums!
-rgt

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This started out creepy/disturbing, veered close to -J territory, and finished back in creepy/disturbing. I was imagining Circus patrons listening to pickled punks singing in harmony; that's definitely a disquieting act.

The reminder that Fuller is no longer part of the Circus also points up the fact that (as far as I can recall) we've never actually learned how or why that happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the description, but when I started the interview I felt afraid that it would lose me. The tone of their speech felt jarring(hah) compared to what had been presented in the main article. But this pulled through that. "The aborted bastards of Herman Fuller, dear old dad." made me go daaaaaaamn. Overall, it's not easy to square away the disparate tones in this article and make it feel like a cohesive whole. That took some skill. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was… Strangely adorable. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's so much that makes this. The picture is a great start, and then the combination of fun dialogue and horrific backstory makes it entertaining in a way that's perfect for a circus themed article. I would have upvoted for the toilet wine alone.

Foundation Toilet Wine would be a pretty good name for a band.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was suprisingly creepy, yet suprisingly cute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That last line was great…
+1 with a shiny new mech

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First the shock value… and then, they are actually a singing troupe. Wonder how they will be like in the showbiz?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Image Source: https://commons.m.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Human_fetus.jpg

Thanks to my beta readers:

Varaxous
towwl
Ihp
OthellotheCat
ghostchibi
Pecan

And special thanks to LilyFlower for finding the image's source for me, as well as to Zyn, as the first part of the interview was partially inspired by a conversation we had over chat.
